meta.title: 2.4 Reading Real CSS
meta.description: Media queries, hover states, transitions — decoding CSS you will actually encounter.

blocks.prompt: Websites often look different on your phone than on your laptop — different layouts, different font sizes, sometimes different menus. What do you think makes the same CSS work differently on different screen sizes?

blocks.question: If you saw `@media (max-width: 768px)` in a stylesheet, could you explain what it means and when the CSS inside it would take effect?
blocks.answer: This is a media query that activates when the screen (or browser window) is 768 pixels wide or smaller — roughly tablet-sized or smaller. Any CSS rules inside this block only apply when that condition is true. On a wider screen, those rules are ignored. This is how websites adapt their layout for mobile devices.

options: A) On screens 768px or smaller, the grid switches from multiple columns to a single column
B) The grid is always one column wide regardless of screen size
C) On screens larger than 768px, the grid becomes one column
problems.explanation: The `@media (max-width: 768px)` condition means this rule only applies when the screen is 768px wide or smaller. `grid-template-columns: 1fr` sets the grid to a single column. So on mobile/tablet, the layout becomes one column. On desktop (wider than 768px), the grid uses whatever columns are defined outside this media query.

options: A) The link instantly changes from blue to dark blue
B) The link smoothly changes from blue to dark blue over 0.2 seconds
C) The link blinks between blue and dark blue repeatedly
problems.explanation: The `.link:hover` rule changes the color to dark blue when the user hovers. The `transition: color 0.2s ease` on the base rule makes that color change happen smoothly over 0.2 seconds instead of instantly switching. When the user moves the mouse away, it smoothly transitions back to blue.

problems.question: A page looks perfect on desktop but the layout is broken on mobile — cards are overflowing off the right side of the screen. Where in the CSS would you look first?
options: A) Check the JavaScript for mobile-specific bugs
B) Look for `@media` queries that handle smaller screen sizes — there may be a missing or incorrect breakpoint
C) Check if the HTML has mobile-specific tags
problems.explanation: When something looks fine on desktop but breaks on mobile, the issue is almost always in the CSS media queries (or the lack of them). The layout may be using fixed widths that work on desktop but overflow on narrow screens. You would look for `@media` rules that adjust the layout for smaller screens, or check if such rules are missing entirely.

questions: Think about a website you use regularly. Can you identify one thing that probably uses a media query (something that changes between desktop and mobile) and one thing that probably uses a :hover state?
When you encounter a CSS property you do not recognize, what is your strategy for understanding it?
How does knowing about CSS variables change how you might describe a 'change the brand color' request to a developer or AI?

blocks.content: Media queries (`@media`) make CSS conditional — usually based on screen size, which is how responsive design works. Hover states (`:hover`) change styling when the user moves their mouse over an element. Transitions make those changes smooth instead of instant. CSS variables (`var(--name)`) let you define values once and reuse them everywhere. For unknown properties, read the name, read the value, and search if needed — do not memorize.

blocks.prompt: You have now covered the core of CSS — how to read rules, understand spacing, decode layouts, and recognize real-world patterns like media queries and hover states. Time to put it all together. The Level 2 Checkpoint will test your ability to look at CSS and figure out what is going wrong.

reviewQuestions.question: A media query like @media (max-width: 768px) { ... } means:
options: The CSS inside only applies when the screen is 768 pixels wide or narrower
The page will only load on mobile devices
reviewQuestions.explanation: Media queries make CSS conditional. max-width: 768px means the styles apply only on screens up to 768px wide — this is how responsive design works.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's responsive design! Different styles for different screen sizes.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The @media query makes CSS conditional based on screen size. max-width: 768px means the styles inside only apply on screens 768px wide or narrower.

reviewQuestions.question: The :hover pseudo-class in CSS applies styles when:
options: The user moves their mouse over the element
reviewQuestions.explanation: :hover triggers when the mouse pointer moves over an element. It's commonly used for button and link hover effects.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: :hover styles apply when the user moves their mouse over the element. It's the most common way to add interactive visual feedback.
